section.title: Teach Me to Do Your Will
section.sectionRationale: The last and most theologically weighty of the four Davidic laments closing Book V, counted among the traditional seven penitential psalms, this prayer confesses that no one alive is righteous before God even while pleading for rescue from enemies, and closes by asking not merely for safety but for the Spirit's guidance onto level ground.
section.thesis: David appeals to God's own righteousness and faithfulness rather than his own merit, confessing that no one alive is righteous before Him, and moves from crushing darkness through remembered mercy to an urgent plea that God's good Spirit would both rescue him and teach him to do God's will, safety and holiness sought together rather than safety alone.
section.frameNote: Five movements: a plea for righteous mercy admitting universal sinfulness, verses 1-2; crushing darkness described, verses 3-4; hope revived by remembering God's works, verses 5-6; urgent cries for guidance, verses 7-8; and dependence on God's Spirit for both deliverance and vindication, verses 9-12.

verses.text: O LORD, hear my prayer. In Your faithfulness, give ear to my plea; in Your {{tsedeq|righteousness}}, answer me.
Do not bring Your servant into judgment, for no one alive is righteous before You.

units.label: Crushing darkness
units.note: the weight of pursuit and despair

verses.text: For the enemy has pursued my soul, crushing my life to the ground, making me dwell in darkness like those long since dead.
My spirit grows faint within me; my heart is dismayed inside me.

units.label: I remember the days of old
units.note: hope revived by remembering God's works

verses.text: I remember the days of old; I meditate on all Your works; I consider the work of Your hands.
I stretch out my hands to You; my soul thirsts for You like a parched land. Selah
Answer me quickly, O LORD; my spirit fails. Do not hide Your face from me, or I will be like those who descend to the Pit.
Let me hear Your loving devotion in the morning, for I have put my trust in You. Teach me the way I should walk, for to You I lift up my soul.

units.label: Teach me to do Your will
units.note: dependence on God's Spirit for deliverance and vindication

verses.text: Deliver me from my enemies, O LORD; I flee to You for refuge.
Teach me to do Your will, for You are my God. May Your good {{ruach|Spirit}} lead me on level ground.
For the sake of Your name, O LORD, revive me. In Your righteousness, bring my soul out of trouble.
And in Your loving devotion, cut off my enemies. Destroy all who afflict me, for I am Your servant.

tsedeq.gloss: Righteousness, rightness. David appeals not to his own righteousness, which he denies possessing in verse 2, but to God's own righteous character, His faithfulness to act rightly by His own name and word, the very ground of the psalm's confidence.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, breath, wind. David asks that God's good ruach lead him on level ground, one of the clearest Old Testament pleas for the Spirit's personal, moral guidance, anticipating the New Testament's fuller teaching on the Spirit leading believers into righteousness.

basic.context: Pursued and crushed to the ground by an enemy, David does not appeal to his own righteousness, which he explicitly denies, but to God's faithfulness and righteous character, moving from remembered mercy to an urgent, twofold plea for both rescue and moral guidance.
basic.hermeneutics: The psalm describes David's specific crisis of pursuit and near despair; it prescribes the pattern of appealing to God's own character rather than personal merit, of reviving hope through deliberate remembrance of God's past works, and of seeking guidance in righteousness alongside safety rather than safety alone.
basic.originalLanguages: David appeals to God's tsedeq, righteousness, not his own, confessing plainly that no one alive is righteous before God, verse 2. His climactic request asks that God's good ruach, Spirit, lead him on level ground, one of the Old Testament's clearest pleas for the Spirit's personal, moral guidance.
basic.typology: David's confession that no one alive is righteous before God, verse 2, anticipates Paul's citation of similar Psalms texts in Romans 3:10-20 to establish universal guilt, while his appeal to God's Spirit to lead him on level ground anticipates the New Testament's promise of the Spirit's ongoing guidance into truth, John 16:13.
basic.christ: David's plea, do not bring Your servant into judgment, for no one alive is righteous before You, verse 2, finds its answer in Christ, in whom believers are not brought into judgment, Romans 8:1, because His own perfect righteousness is credited to those who trust Him, and His Spirit now leads them, as David asked, into the way they should walk.
basic.god: God is shown as righteous and faithful, hearing prayer not because of the psalmist's merit but because of His own character, personally present to revive, guide, and deliver, and committed to leading His servant not merely to safety but into obedience to His will.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 3:10-20 develops this psalm's confession that no one alive is righteous before God into the doctrine of universal sin. Romans 8:1 answers David's plea not to be brought into judgment, now fulfilled for those in Christ. John 16:13 fulfills the plea for God's Spirit to lead into the right way.
basic.oneStory: David's honest confession that no one is righteous before God, paired with his plea for the Spirit's guidance into right living, anticipates the gospel's full answer, no condemnation for those in Christ and the indwelling Spirit who leads believers, as David asked, on level ground into God's will.

context.literary: A penitential and imploring lament in five movements, plea admitting universal sin, verses 1-2, crushing darkness, verses 3-4, hope revived by remembrance, verses 5-6, urgent cries for guidance, verses 7-8, and dependence on God's Spirit for deliverance and vindication, verses 9-12.
context.historical: Attributed to David, its vocabulary of pursuit, darkness, and pleading fits seasons such as flight from Saul, 1 Samuel 19-26, or from Absalom, 2 Samuel 15-18, both placing David in the arid Judean wilderness where dry land and threatened death were vivid realities, not mere metaphors.
context.canonical: The last of the closing Davidic lament cluster, Psalms 140-143, and traditionally counted among the seven penitential psalms, 6, 32, 38, 51, 102, 130, and this one, it turns the sequence's honesty about danger into honesty about universal sinfulness before God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To appeal to God's own righteous and faithful character rather than personal merit, to revive failing hope through deliberate remembrance of God's past works, and to ask for both deliverance from enemies and moral guidance into God's will together.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes David's specific danger and his confession of universal human unrighteousness; it prescribes appealing to God's character rather than self-righteousness, actively remembering God's past faithfulness in present distress, and seeking to be led into obedience alongside safety.

debates.question: How can David ask God to answer him in His righteousness, verse 1, when he confesses that no one alive is righteous before God, verse 2?

views.label: God's righteousness here means His faithfulness to His own name and covenant character, doing right by who He has revealed Himself to be, rather than rewarding David's own moral merit
views.case: Verse 11 pairs righteousness with for the sake of Your name, suggesting God's righteousness is His commitment to act consistently with His own honor and promises, not a verdict on David's personal innocence.

views.label: God's righteousness includes His covenant faithfulness to those who trust Him, so that answering in righteousness means vindicating the one who has fled to Him for refuge, verse 9, even though that one is a sinner
views.case: Throughout the psalm David's only ground for hope is his trust in and refuge in God, verses 8-9, so God's righteousness is exercised toward those who rely on Him rather than earned by their own righteousness, anticipating justification by faith.

debates.bearing: Both readings preserve the psalm's honest confession of universal sin while grounding all hope in God's own character and covenant faithfulness rather than human merit, the same logic Paul later develops fully in Romans 3.

study.originalLanguages: David appeals to God's tsedeq, righteousness, not his own, plainly confessing that no one alive is righteous before Him, verse 2. His climactic plea asks that God's good ruach, Spirit, lead him on level ground, one of the Old Testament's clearest requests for the Spirit's personal, moral guidance.

typology.type: No one alive is righteous before You, verse 2
typology.fulfillment: Paul's citation of similar Psalms texts establishing universal human guilt, Romans 3:10-20.

typology.type: May Your good Spirit lead me on level ground, verse 10
typology.fulfillment: The Spirit's ongoing guidance of believers into all truth, John 16:13.

study.christ: David's plea, do not bring Your servant into judgment, for no one alive is righteous before You, verse 2, is answered in Christ, in whom there is now no condemnation, Romans 8:1, His own righteousness credited to all who trust Him, and His Spirit now leads believers, as David asked, into the way they should walk.
study.god: God is righteous and faithful, hearing prayer because of His own character rather than the psalmist's merit, personally present to revive, guide, and deliver, and committed to leading His servant not merely to safety but into obedience to His will.

crossReferences.note: Develops this psalm's confession that no one alive is righteous before God into the full doctrine of universal human sin.
Answers David's plea not to be brought into judgment, now realized for those in Christ Jesus.
Fulfills the plea for God's Spirit to lead into truth and the right way, verse 10.

study.oneStory: David's honest confession that no one is righteous before God, paired with his plea for the Spirit's guidance into right living, anticipates the gospel's full answer, no condemnation for those in Christ and the indwelling Spirit who leads believers, as David asked, on level ground into God's will.

sermon.title: How to Argue with God
sermon.why: Piper works through Psalm 143 verse by verse, showing what David wanted, how he prayed, and how he argued his case before God on the basis of God's own righteousness and name rather than his own merit, directly expounding this passage's central logic.

quiz.q: On what basis does David ask God to answer him in verse 1?

options.text: God's own faithfulness and righteousness, not David's personal merit.
options.note: David explicitly appeals to God's character, in Your faithfulness, in Your righteousness, rather than his own worthiness.

options.text: David's flawless record of obedience to God's law.
options.note: Verse 2 explicitly denies any such flawless record, confessing that no one alive is righteous before God.

options.text: The size of David's army and political power.
options.note: The appeal is entirely to God's character, not to any human strength or power.

options.text: A formal legal contract David had drawn up with God.
options.note: The appeal is relational and covenantal, resting on God's revealed character, not a formal contract.

quiz.q: What does David confess about humanity in verse 2?

options.text: That no one alive is righteous before God.
options.note: This blunt confession of universal sinfulness grounds the psalm's appeal to grace rather than merit.

options.text: That some people are righteous enough to earn God's favor.
options.note: The verse states plainly the opposite, that no one alive is righteous before God.

options.text: That only his enemies are sinful, while he himself is blameless.
options.note: David includes himself, asking God not to bring Your servant into judgment, in this universal confession.

options.text: That righteousness is impossible even for God to define.
options.note: The verse concerns human unrighteousness before God's standard, not any ambiguity in God's own righteousness.

quiz.q: How does David revive his hope in verses 5-6?

options.text: By deliberately remembering the days of old and meditating on all God's works and the work of His hands.
options.note: David actively recalls God's past faithfulness as the means of reviving present hope amid despair.

options.text: By ignoring the past entirely and focusing only on his own present feelings.
options.note: The passage describes intentional remembrance of the past, not avoidance of it.

options.text: By seeking advice from foreign kings.
options.note: David's hope is revived through remembering God's works, not through consulting other rulers.

options.text: By convincing himself that his troubles were not really that serious.
options.note: David does not minimize his troubles; he reorients hope through remembering God's faithfulness while still describing genuine distress.

quiz.q: What twofold request does David make in verses 9-10?

options.text: Deliverance from his enemies and instruction to do God's will, led by God's good Spirit.
options.note: David seeks both safety and moral guidance together, not safety alone.

options.text: Only wealth and comfortable living, with no concern for obedience.
options.note: The request explicitly includes, teach me to do Your will, moral guidance alongside safety.

options.text: Only revenge against his enemies with no request for guidance.
options.note: While enemies are mentioned, verse 10 explicitly requests guidance in doing God's will, not merely revenge.

options.text: A request to be left alone by both God and his enemies.
options.note: David actively seeks God's presence and guidance, not distance or isolation from God.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's confession of universal unrighteousness connect to the New Testament?

options.text: It anticipates Paul's teaching that no one is righteous before God and that in Christ there is now no condemnation for those who trust Him.
options.note: Romans 3:10-20 and Romans 8:1 develop exactly this psalm's confession and its resolution in Christ.

options.text: It has no relationship to New Testament teaching on sin and grace.
options.note: Paul directly draws on similar Psalms texts to build his doctrine of universal sin and grace in Christ.

options.text: It suggests that only Gentiles, not Israelites, are unrighteous before God.
options.note: The confession is universal, no one alive, not limited to any single people group.

options.text: It implies that David alone, uniquely among all people, was righteous.
options.note: David explicitly includes himself as a servant needing mercy, not an exception to universal unrighteousness.

questions: David appeals to God's righteousness and faithfulness rather than his own merit. Where are you tempted to approach God on the basis of your own performance rather than His character and grace?

David feels crushed to the ground and dwelling in darkness, yet still turns to prayer. What crushing weight are you carrying right now that needs to be honestly brought to God rather than carried alone?
David deliberately remembers God's past works to revive present hope. What specific past evidence of God's faithfulness in your own life could you deliberately recall this week?

David asks not only to be rescued but to be taught God's will and led by God's Spirit on level ground. Are you seeking safety alone from your current difficulty, or also asking God to shape your character through it?

David confesses that no one alive is righteous before God, yet still boldly asks for mercy. How does knowing that Christ answers this exact confession, no condemnation for those in Him, change how you approach God today?

translationNotes.note: No one alive is righteous before You is one of the Old Testament's clearest statements of universal human sinfulness, later drawn on by Paul in Romans 3:10-20 to establish that all have sinned.

The Pit renders a Hebrew word, bor, that can mean both a literal cistern or grave, and figuratively Sheol, the realm of the dead, the same word used of literal cisterns turned into prisons, Jeremiah 38:6.
I flee to You for refuge carries a footnote alternate rendering, in You I take cover, both expressing David's total reliance on God rather than his own strength for protection.

No em or en dashes were present in the source text for this psalm. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming John Piper's 'How to Argue with God' at desiringgod.org, Scripture Psalm 143.
